speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll six.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hofman, yes. You were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. In the end it was survived 14 people in our town. It was 1,500, and we survived 14 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you returned, you decide-- what happened when you returned to the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was looking at first like we were the worst in the world. But they were afraid for their properties, for this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided not to stay there. How can you stay in a cemetery?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Biala.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you-- and how long were you in Biala?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was waiting till-- then I went to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and how long were you in Miedzyrzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was till the-- till the Germans liberated-- the Russians liberated Lodz. I went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was after May '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did you meet Pola Roiseman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pola was with me in the woods with her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was one of the people that was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one of the nine, 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is Pola Roiseman today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pola Hofman, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years are you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're married 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- how long where you in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked till-- till '46

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there? A year, a half a year, three months-- how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, maybe half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I been-- I was a [INAUDIBLE] also. Because it was no home and no food. So we had to go. It-- it was-- I-- I didn't have money. And they came, two nieces came from-- from Auschwitz. I had to take care of them because I couldn't let them go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the nieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. Chana and Esther. Unfortunately, Esther passed away a couple years ago. I had to go-- I struggled to-- to keep them alive. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who remained from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remained-- me and Esther and Chana. These three people remained. My sister's two nieces and-- and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole-- your whole family-- what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Treblinka, all of them. In 1942, the first day of [INAUDIBLE] That's a Saturday night. No, Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all your family was killed in Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, what year were you in Lodz that you were a half a year there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, after the-- after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- and you went to Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945. And where in Germany did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me the conditions and what you did in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting and waiting for to be able to go to Israel or to America. There was a camp there, a Jewish camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a camp, Neu Freimann. Outskirts of Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this camp was run by Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Jewish. Yeah, Jews. The-- the American Army had the control. They used to get food from the UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: The UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were there, did you go to school? Did you work? What did you do in-- between 1946--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be honest with you. I couldn't do nothing. I was very upset from what happened. Since I was liberated the first day, in-- in a koshmar. What happened to my family-- it was such a close, good family. It was example for the city for that little town. And I couldn't go outside from the-- from the-- the neighborhood where I was in Germany, see fathers, mothers, grandpas, children. I was very, very upset constantly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so it was a difficult time. How did you cope with it? Did you get some medication? Did you get medical help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't need it. I was I just-- I was in a koshmar all my life since I was liberated, but I didn't need any medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your wife, Pola?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pola she was a-- my son was born in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your son that was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: George.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was George--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish his name is Gedale [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Gedale or George, born? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in-- the end of '97.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so what was happening in Germany? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting and waiting to get there to Israel because we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was helping you with that to go to Israel or to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- it was agreement between the American government and with the UNRRA, with [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August the 6th, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my uncle, my mother's sister. Her name was [? Leer. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: To New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn, New York. And he was Max.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max. Mordecai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mordecai. And what happened then? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I couldn't get a job. I was struggling. But the-- the HIAS helped us out a little bit. They chipped in something. And I felt terrible. I couldn't even stand it to get charity. I would-- it came now down to charity. But [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], that's what I think. And I-- I tried to-- finally, I got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- pickling corned beef, provision. I learned the line very fast. But I started with $30 and I went up to $50. That's it. But $50 was not enough. Then I started the other job and I went up to $85. I worked on a lot of-- I worked till '53 probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have another daughter in-- four years-- four years younger than my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn. In Brooklyn Women's Hospital. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Sherry. She is married now. She has a husband and two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was she born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And now-- so you had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sherry has two children, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you tried to support them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my son has now three-- three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened while you were trying to get the different jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job for $30 and went up to $50, and I couldn't go for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a union job and I was working six, seven days a week. Finally, I went in, I got another job for $85 with 40 hours, a union place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was working for a-- for-- in a-- then I said to myself, that job was finished for $85, and I had to go another place. And the guy I kept for two hours and he sended me home. Then I said to myself, I'm not going nowhere to work. I said to my wife, Pola, don't say yes but don't say no. I'm going to go in business for myself. I'm going to buy a kosher butcher shop because my friend had kosher butcher shops and made a nice living. And I don't want to be pushed around by nobody. And this was happened. I bought a butcher shop for a few dollars. He gave me a mortgage, and I took in a refugee partner. And I worked. I was staying in there for about two years. I made a nice living. At plain work, I made $60. I made in the butcher shop $100. It was very good. I was a king. Then I didn't like it, the location. And that's it. And I bought another store in Brooklyn. The first one was in the Bronx. The other one is in Brooklyn. I used to live in Brooklyn. And I took in another partner. A very capable man. And I made already $300 a week. But I still-- still didn't like it, the kosher butcher business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went in in the building line. What happened? I was an organizer for a shul in Brooklyn, me and another 15 guys. What was the name of the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seaview Jewish Center. Me and another 15 guys, we was-- we had a shed for the shul. But then they come in. People moved in. They're going to build a shul. Then the lawyer fixed up and discussed with the auctioneer. And he said come to the auction, pick up a parcel. And we will sell you for the up-sale price and we'll build you-- we'll build a shul. And I was at that time in that meeting. I was on the board. And I heard this story. I keep my ears on. What happened? Then I said to him-- after that meeting, I said, where is the place? He said, here's a telephone. Call up, and they will get a booklet from the parcels what is on the auction from the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the book and I looked around. And I didn't know anything by that book. They were-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] But I said to my partner, you want to go in business? Now is the time. Let's go in business in the building line. He was listening to me. He was a carpenter but it didn't help anything. Another guy told one nephew. And we went in. We looked around. We didn't know anything but I went to the auction and I bought land. How much anybody wanted to pay, I paid more, more, and more. And I bought it. The start was bad. I was a silent partner in butcher shop and they was the two running the business. It was not so good. Then I went in myself. I quit the butcher shop. I sold to my partner. And then, thank God, I-- I came out good. I was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Any more questions? And where are you living now? Where-- where did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved from Brooklyn to-- to Marlboro, New Jersey. My son lives there, five minutes away. As you're getting older, you have to stay near a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me the names of your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, Georgie, you know. The wife, Lori, you know. She's a very, very capable girl. She-- she plays piano. She's teaching him piano, and she speaks French. She's capable. The oldest one is Jacob, my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob is gonna to be 16 in July the 8th. That's next one is Michael. He's going to be 15-- no 14 in-- in-- in February. And the little one is Philip. He is gonna be-- he's gonna be 11 next April.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your daughter, Sherry?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has a son who's 17, Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sherry, and what is her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred Sztul. And the oldest boy is 17, past 17 in March, a very capable boy. And the younger one is Josh, Yeshia, And he is-- he is passed 11 in February. Shall I tell you the Jewish name for the kids?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you would like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like it. George's is Yankev Moshe. And the second one is [? Mayer ?] Enoch. And the little one is [? Efraim ?] [? Buman. ?] And Sherry's is [? Tsvi ?] Elie And the other one is Yeshia [? Benjamin ?] The three children from my-- my-- from my-- for my real brother, and for my wife's brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now are you here for the winter in Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Florida in winter. I bought my condominium in 1973 when I was for vacation. I-- I bought it and I wanted to sell. I couldn't sell it and I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am semi. I like to be something on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something that you would like to tell, to leave for the generations? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to talk about the Polish people. Is there time now? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right. I-- Wasla Shpura, he was the messenger-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say? We are at the end of the tape. What would you like to say to leave for the generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for the generation. [SIGH] For the generation, I am going to say-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's very painful for me to talk now about it. I've been going through the whole executions, through all the executions, and all-- all of the transports. I've seen with my eyes the more than a million kids. The blood is not dry from them. And they're cursing the world. Why they did so to them? Why they didn't stand up for them? They-- just because they were Jewish. They got a such a miserable end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody when he takes his child on his hand let him remember that million Jewish kids got killed for no reason because they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't finish. Let me talk. Let me talk a few things more. And I cannot forgive President Roosevelt. He was a good man for the country, but for the Jewish people he didn't care. He know it was going on. And the world know it. Nobody opened up the mouth. And the Pope, Pious XXII, he know it. He helped the-- the Gestapo. And this I cannot-- since then, I cannot be-- I've been in a koshmar. Burns me terrible because what they did to my family, such a close family, and to the Jewish people. Such a miserable end, worse than dogs, worse than cats. And this everybody should remember. Why we had-- we lived in a country like Poland. I could have stayed, we bought from them, we helped them. We did for them everything. And they was so bad. If not for Polish people, I wouldn't survive. But how many was there?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? They didn't need their help. If they wouldn't show [INAUDIBLE] for the Jews. Mr. Hofman--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story with us. We appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it? Well, there is still got to go? No? All right. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hofman, please tell me about this photograph from 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my house, and I was living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city is this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Konstantyn near the River Bug and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is in front of this house? I stay in the front. And my friend, a girl. And my-- children from the neighborhood, including my niece. And my brother is going there always to-- to his house. His house was in the back of this one. You also wanted to mention the five people who were your neighbors and who helped you during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town. Can you give me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasla Shpura, with his family, he helped me. He helped about 10 people, maybe more. But he-- after the liberation, the Polish people killed him, and the police wouldn't let them bury on the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was helped Jews. Now the next one, it was Josef-- Josef Mazurek. He helped me in the neighborhood. And [? Duderush-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Dudek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dudek. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jacob

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob [? Scavron ?] and his son-in-law-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Antosh? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Antosh. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Richuck. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Richuck, ?] [? Antosh ?] [? Richuck. ?] And they helped us, but they was very afraid for the neighbors to help Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Hofman, who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, my mother-in-law, my daughter, my son. On my daughter's wedding night, this was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in November 24, 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hofman, who are these people, and what date, and what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Louis Hofman, and this is his-- his lovely wife, Pola. And this is taken, I think, in San Francisco in 1982 or in 1980, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Hofman, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife, Pola Hofman, and the first grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Sztul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken was in my house after the-- after the Seder probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this would be probably in-- 16 and 1/2 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr. Hofman, Who is in this photograph? From the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left, this is the picture from my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please give me the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Made in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this must be about 1976. And this is from the-- from the right side, Pola Hofman, Louis Hofman, Georgie Hofman, Lori Hofman. And Jacob Hofman, Michael Hofman, and Philip Hofman. The next one is Sherry Sztul, my daughter, Fred Sztul, Henry Sztul, and Josh Sztul. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date, May 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, May 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which date? The picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was made in-- 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, this picture is taken in '96.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is '96? I don't know. All right, whenever. [LAUGHS]
